# Transformer Expressivity and Formal Models of Computation

The unique hard attention Transformer (UHAT) is a formal abstraction of decoder-only, causally masked Transformers in which each attention head concentrates its entire attention on the single position that maximizes the attention score, with ties broken by selecting the rightmost element. This abstraction is motivated by empirical observations that pretrained Transformers tend to concentrate attention mass on very few positions. Because UHAT subsumes the expressivity of ordinary softmax Transformers operating at fixed numerical precision, it serves as a plausible formal model for reasoning about Transformer capabilities—particularly in regimes involving long contexts and large reasoning problems where precision constraints become binding.

In the UHAT model, each layer consists of an attention block followed by an MLP block. Multi-head attention concatenates the outputs of individual heads and projects them to the model's hidden dimension. The model has access to arbitrary positional embedding vectors for each position within its context window. A central question in analyzing what functions Transformers can compute is whether the number of layers and attention heads must remain bounded as input length grows. Requiring bounded depth and bounded number of heads across all input lengths—while allowing width to grow, for instance to support positional encodings that distinguish unboundedly many positions—constitutes a relatively weak uniformity condition compared to requiring a single fixed-size Transformer for all input lengths. Despite this weakness, this uniformity condition is sufficient to establish tight upper and lower bounds on the computational costs of various reasoning tasks.

### Chain-of-Thought and Extended Computation

The expressivity of Transformers augmented with chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting has deep connections to classical complexity and automata theory. CoT allows a model to generate intermediate reasoning tokens before producing a final answer, effectively extending the computation beyond what a fixed-depth Transformer can achieve in a single forward pass. Each generated token corresponds roughly to an additional computational step, transforming the model from a bounded-depth circuit into something closer to a sequential machine whose total computation scales with the number of intermediate tokens produced. This line of analysis characterizes which function classes are reachable with and without step-by-step reasoning, drawing formal boundaries around what Transformers can and cannot do under various resource constraints.

The distinction between single-pass computation and CoT-augmented computation is not merely theoretical. It maps onto a practical architectural choice: whether to rely on a model's fixed forward pass to produce an answer directly, or to scaffold additional reasoning through intermediate token generation. The formal results suggest that many tasks requiring sequential or iterative reasoning—such as evaluating compositions of functions, simulating automata, or performing multi-step arithmetic—exceed the capacity of bounded-depth Transformers in a single pass but become tractable when the model is permitted to generate a sufficient number of intermediate steps.

### Implications for Formal Complexity Bounds

The UHAT framework enables precise complexity-theoretic characterizations of Transformer computation. By fixing the formal model and varying the resources available—depth, number of heads, context length, and whether CoT is permitted—one can map Transformer expressivity onto well-studied complexity classes. Bounded-depth, bounded-head UHATs without CoT correspond to restricted circuit classes, while the addition of CoT shifts the model's power toward classes associated with sequential computation. These results provide a principled basis for understanding why certain tasks are easy or hard for Transformers, independent of training dynamics or data distribution, and offer guidance on when architectural augmentations like CoT are theoretically necessary rather than merely helpful.

## See Also

- [[z20260318-023|Thinking Tools and Reasoning Augmentation for LLM Agents]] — contrasts the formal analysis of chain-of-thought as extending Transformer expressivity with the practical implementation of reasoning augmentation through thinking tools, which provide a structured mechanism for generating intermediate reasoning tokens in agentic workflows
- [[z20260318-021|LLM Agent Prompting Paradigms: Function Calling and ReAct]] — applies to the practical manifestation of intermediate reasoning in agent systems, where ReAct's thought-action-observation loop can be understood as an empirical instantiation of the extended computation that CoT provides in the formal expressivity framework
- [[z20260318-026|Reinforcement Learning for Communication Policy Optimization]] — contrasts learned communication policies that optimize multi-step agent interactions with the formal characterization of multi-step reasoning in Transformers, both addressing how sequential computation extends the capabilities of bounded-capacity models
